description: Port all significant features from agent-core (openJiuwen) to Orbiter: security guardrails, advanced workflow engine, RAG/retrieval pipeline, context engine enhancements, enhanced memory system, typed agent rails, task management controller, deep agent toolkit, memory evolution algorithms, operator pattern with self-optimization, and full DeepAgent example port (Epic 11). Two new packages (orbiter-guardrail, orbiter-retrieval), rest extends existing packages. Zero regressions on all ~2,900 existing tests.

userStories.title: Research rails architecture (Epic 6)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a design document for the typed rails system so that it extends HookManager without breaking existing hook behavior.
acceptanceCriteria: Read agent-core's rail/base.py — AgentCallbackEvent, typed inputs, AgentRail ABC, @rail decorator
Read Orbiter's hooks.py (HookPoint, HookManager, Hook type alias) and how hooks are called in agent.py
Produce docs/design/rails-design.md covering: typed event inputs, priority ordering, cross-rail state, retry mechanism, integration with existing HookManager
Key decision documented: Rails extend hooks (new optional typed interface) vs. parallel system
Confirm all existing hook_manager.add(HookPoint.X, my_func) calls continue to work unchanged
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Epic 6 is foundational — guardrails (Epic 1) depend on this hook extension. Research phase only, no code changes.

userStories.title: Typed event input models for rails (Epic 6)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want Pydantic models for each hook event's data so that hooks have structured, validated input.
acceptanceCriteria: New file rail_types.py in packages/orbiter-core/src/orbiter/ (max 200 lines)
InvokeInputs model: input: str, messages: list[Any] | None, result: Any | None
ModelCallInputs model: messages: list[Any], tools: list[dict] | None, response: Any | None, usage: Any | None
ToolCallInputs model: tool_name: str, arguments: dict[str, Any], result: Any | None, metadata: Any | None
RailContext model: agent: Any, event: HookPoint, inputs: InvokeInputs | ModelCallInputs | ToolCallInputs, extra: dict[str, Any]
All are Pydantic models (not frozen — hooks may need to mutate inputs)
Tests in test_rail_types.py for model creation and validation
Google docstrings on all public classes
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Rail ABC with priority and retry (Epic 6)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a Rail abstract class with priority ordering and retry capability so that lifecycle guards are structured.
acceptanceCriteria: New file rail.py in packages/orbiter-core/src/orbiter/ (max 200 lines)
Rail ABC with: name: str, priority: int = 50, abstract async def handle(self, ctx: RailContext) -> RailAction | None
RailAction StrEnum: CONTINUE, SKIP, RETRY, ABORT
RetryRequest dataclass: delay: float = 0.0, max_retries: int = 1, reason: str = ''
RailAbortError exception inheriting OrbiterError
Tests for each action type in test_rail.py
Google docstrings on all public APIs
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: RailManager with priority ordering + cross-rail state (Epic 6)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a RailManager that runs rails in priority order with a shared extra dict so that rails can coordinate.
acceptanceCriteria: RailManager class added to rail.py: add(rail), remove(rail), clear()
async run(event: HookPoint, **data) -> RailAction: builds RailContext, runs rails sorted by priority ascending, returns first non-CONTINUE action
extra: dict[str, Any] shared across all rails in same invocation via RailContext
Compatible with HookManager: RailManager can be registered as a single hook on HookManager
Existing hooks registered directly on HookManager are unaffected
Tests for priority ordering, cross-rail extra dict, abort propagation
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Agent integration for rails + backward compatibility (Epic 6)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want rails integrated into Agent with full backward compatibility verification.
acceptanceCriteria: Agent constructor gains optional rails: list[Rail] | None = None parameter
If rails provided, creates RailManager and registers it as hooks on the agent's hook_manager
Agent with no rails behaves exactly as before (no performance or behavior change)
Agent with both traditional hooks and rails: both execute correctly
All existing orbiter-core tests pass unchanged (run uv run pytest packages/orbiter-core/tests/)
Integration test: Rail that aborts on specific tool name
Integration test: Rail that retries on model error
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

Read Orbiter's hooks.py and the new rail.py from previous stories

Document how guardrails interact with existing hooks and rails without breaking execution order

userStories.title: BaseGuardrail with HookManager integration (Epic 1)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a base guardrail class that registers itself as hooks on an Agent's HookManager so that guardrails run automatically during agent execution.
acceptanceCriteria: BaseGuardrail class: backend: GuardrailBackend | None, events: list[str]
attach(agent) method: registers async hooks on agent's hook_manager for each event
detach(agent) method: removes hooks
async detect(event, **data) -> GuardrailResult: calls backend.analyze() if backend set, else returns safe
When GuardrailResult.is_safe is False and risk_level >= HIGH, raises GuardrailError
Existing hooks on agent are NOT disturbed — guardrail hooks append to the list
Tests with mock agent + mock backend verifying hook registration and detection flow
All ~2,900 existing orbiter tests still pass

Tests pass

analyze(data) checks data['messages'] for latest user message against patterns
Returns RiskAssessment with appropriate risk_level based on match confidence

userStories.title: Message offloading processor (Epic 4)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a processor that replaces oversized messages with [[OFFLOAD: handle=<id>]] markers so that context window stays within budget.
acceptanceCriteria: MessageOffloader(ContextProcessor) with event 'pre_llm_call' in processor.py
Constructor: max_message_size: int = 10000 (character limit per message)
process() scans ctx.state['history'], replaces messages exceeding limit with marker containing unique handle ID
Stores original content in ctx.state['offloaded_messages'] dict keyed by handle ID
Does NOT modify system messages (only user/assistant/tool messages)
Tests: message within limit untouched, oversized replaced, original stored
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Reload tool for offloaded content (Epic 4)

acceptanceCriteria: reload_offloaded(handle: str) -> str tool function added to tools.py
Looks up handle in context state's offloaded_messages dict
Returns full original content if found, error message if not

Tests verifying reload of offloaded content and error for unknown handle

Tests pass

userStories.title: Dialogue compression processor (Epic 4)

acceptanceCriteria: DialogueCompressor(ContextProcessor) with event 'pre_llm_call'

process() identifies consecutive tool-call/tool-result message sequences in history
If chain exceeds min_tool_chain_length, replaces with SystemMessage summary
Falls back to simple concatenation if no summarizer provided

Tests pass

process() parses history into rounds (user message -> assistant response, including tool calls)

userStories.title: AES-256 encryption at rest (Epic 5)

acceptanceCriteria: EncryptedMemoryStore wrapper class implementing MemoryStore protocol
Constructor: store: MemoryStore, key: bytes (32-byte AES key)
add() encrypts item.content before delegating (AES-256-GCM: nonce + tag + ciphertext)
get() and search() decrypt content after retrieval
Key derivation helper: derive_key(password, salt) -> bytes using PBKDF2
Optional dependency [encryption] in pyproject.toml (uses cryptography package)
Tests: encrypt -> store -> retrieve -> decrypt roundtrip, wrong key fails

Tests pass

userStories.title: LLM-based memory deduplication (Epic 5)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want LLM-powered deduplication that detects semantically similar memories and decides ADD, DELETE, or MERGE.
acceptanceCriteria: MemUpdateChecker class: takes model: str (optional, for LLM calls)
async check(new_item, existing: list[MemoryItem]) -> MergeResult
UpdateDecision StrEnum: ADD, DELETE, MERGE, SKIP
MergeResult dataclass: decision, merged_content: str | None, delete_ids: list[str]
If model provided: sends new + top-5 similar to LLM for comparison
If no model: falls back to exact content match (matching existing LongTermMemory behavior)
Integration with LongTermMemory.add() — optional checker parameter
Tests with MockProvider for LLM-based dedup and fallback behavior

Tests pass

userStories.title: Safe AST expression evaluator (Epic 2)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a security-hardened expression evaluator for workflow branch conditions so that user-provided expressions cannot execute arbitrary code.
acceptanceCriteria: New file _internal/expression.py in orbiter-core (max 200 lines)
evaluate_expression(expr: str, variables: dict[str, Any]) -> Any function
AST-based parsing via ast.parse(mode='eval')
Blocks: dunder access, imports, dangerous function calls, attribute access on dangerous types
Limits: MAX_EXPRESSION_LENGTH=500, MAX_AST_DEPTH=10, MAX_COLLECTION_SIZE=1000
Normalizes: && -> and, || -> or, true -> True, false -> False
Supports: comparisons, boolean ops, arithmetic, string ops, dict/list indexing
Tests with valid expressions and attack vectors (injection, DOS, dunder access)
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Text chunking strategies (Epic 3)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want configurable text chunking so that documents are split into retrieval-friendly chunks.
acceptanceCriteria: Chunker ABC: def chunk(self, document: Document) -> list[Chunk]
CharacterChunker: splits by character count with configurable chunk_size and chunk_overlap
ParagraphChunker: splits at paragraph boundaries, respects chunk_size limit

acceptanceCriteria: Create docs/porting-guide/index.md
Section 1: Introduction — what agent-core (openJiuwen) is, why features were ported, link to agent-core-vs-orbiter-analysis.md
Section 2: Architecture comparison — agent-core's single-package structure vs Orbiter's 15+ package monorepo, diagram showing which agent-core directories map to which Orbiter packages
Section 3: Design philosophy differences — agent-core's inheritance-heavy + Pregel channels vs Orbiter's composition + Swarm transfers
Section 4: Package mapping table — every agent-core top-level directory → Orbiter package(s)
Section 5: Per-epic guide links — table linking to all 10 docs/porting-guide/*.md files with one-line summaries
Section 6: Acknowledgments — credit to the openJiuwen project and its contributors
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Final documentation story. Requires all other docs stories (US-076 through US-085) to be complete first.
